text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Margaret Yuen
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Attending Oncologist: [on file]
Visit Type: Established Patient Follow-Up / Restaging Review
Department: Medical Oncology, Thoracic Oncology Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Yuen is a 63-year-old Chinese-American woman with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, now presenting for follow-up after completion of cycle 3 of second-line Paraplatin/pem/Avastin. She returns today for review of restaging imaging, toxicity assessment, and discussion of the path forward.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Yuen's oncologic history is significant for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma diagnosed approximately 18 months ago. At the time of diagnosis, she was deemed unresectable due to multi-station N2 nodal involvement at stations 4R and 7. She declined concurrent chemoradiation given her underlying COPD (GOLD Stage I) and, after extensive multidisciplinary discussion, elected to proceed with upfront Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily. She had an excellent initial response, with her primary right upper lobe mass decreasing from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm at approximately 9 months. Unfortunately, restaging imaging at approximately 14 months showed unequivocal progression: the primary mass grew back to 3.2 cm, the right paratracheal nodes re-enlarged, a new small right pleural effusion appeared, and brain MRI revealed two new enhancing lesions in the right frontal lobe (1.1 cm) and right cerebellar hemisphere (0.8 cm), consistent with intracranial metastases.

Liquid biopsy at that time (Guardant360) and subsequent tissue NGS (FoundationOne CDx) confirmed acquired resistance, with EGFR T790M detected alongside the original L858R. Notably, a bypass track resistance mechanism was also identified on plasma ctDNA and confirmed on repeat tissue biopsy -- MET amplification (copy number 6.8 on subsequent re-biopsy) was later identified as the dominant resistance driver.

She was referred to radiation oncology and underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) with a frameless Varian Edge system to both brain metastases (right frontal: 20 Gy x 1 fraction; right cerebellar: 22 Gy x 1 fraction) approximately 15 months after her original diagnosis. She tolerated SRS well, with only transient grade 1 fatigue and headache managed with a brief course of dexamethasone and acetaminophen.

Following SRS, osi was discontinued and she was started on second-line systemic chemotherapy with Paraplatin (AUC 5) plus Alimta (500 mg/m2) plus Avastin (15 mg/kg), all administered intravenously on day 1 of a 21-day cycle. Folic acid 400 mcg orally daily and vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks were initiated per standard Alimta supplementation protocol.

She has now completed 3 cycles of this regimen. Her most recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain demonstrated a favorable response: the right upper lobe primary mass has decreased from 3.2 cm at progression to 2.4 cm -- a roughly 25% reduction from the progression nadir. The right paratracheal lymphadenopathy has also decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm, and the right pleural effusion that was present at progression has resolved completely. Brain MRI is very encouraging: the right frontal SRS-treated lesion has shrunk from 1.1 cm to 0.6 cm, and the cerebellar lesion from 0.8 cm to 0.4 cm, both consistent with a brisk post-SRS treatment response, though radiation necrosis cannot be entirely excluded at this point.

Today she reports that her energy has noticeably improved compared to the time of disease progression. She notes significantly reduced shortness of breath -- she is now able to perform her activities of daily living and walk around her neighborhood without stopping, which she was unable to do during the progression period. Headaches, which were a concerning symptom at the time brain metastases were discovered, have largely resolved. She is sleeping better and her appetite has returned. She acknowledges persistent but manageable nausea, particularly in the 24-48 hours following infusion, and mild fatigue that improves by day 5-6 of each cycle. She denies any fever, chills, hemoptysis, chest pain, or new neurologic symptoms including no new focal weakness, vision changes, or difficulties with coordination or balance. She has not had any bleeding episodes. Bowel habits have been somewhat loose at times but nothing that meets criteria for grade 2 diarrhea. Peripheral neuropathy has not been a significant complaint through the first three cycles.

Her daughter, who lives nearby and accompanies her to most appointments, reports that her mother's mood and engagement have improved substantially since the chemotherapy appeared to be working. She was tearful at prior visits when discussing progression, but today she is in noticeably better spirits.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Mild residual fatigue (grade 1), intermittent nausea in early post-infusion days (grade 1-2, well-controlled), mild hair thinning (grade 1 alopecia), occasional loose stools, mild bilateral first toe paronychia (residual from prior Tagrisso, slowly resolving). Headaches nearly resolved.

Negative: No hemoptysis, no pleuritis, no significant dyspnea at rest, no chest pain, no palpitations, no edema, no abdominal pain, no hematuria, no epistaxis or gingival bleeding, no confusion or memory changes, no focal neurologic deficits, no new skin rash (the grade 1-2 acneiform rash from osi has nearly fully resolved since discontinuation), no fever or signs of infection.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma (right upper lobe), as above
2. Hypertension -- well-controlled on lisinopril
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- well-controlled, HbA1c 6.9% at last check
4. COPD, GOLD Stage I -- managed with PRN albuterol inhaler; no recent exacerbations
5. History of EGFR TKI-related grade 2 acneiform rash and grade 1 paronychia (resolved post-osi discontinuation)
6. Two brain metastases, status post SRS (right frontal and right cerebellar), responding post-treatment

SOCIAL HISTORY

Retired high school chemistry teacher. Non-smoker currently; 20 pack-year history, quit 12 years prior to diagnosis. Lives at home in the community with support from her adult daughter who lives nearby. No significant alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Born and raised in San Francisco, CA; Chinese-American heritage. Supportive family and social network. Actively engaged with the clinical team and asks insightful questions about her care.

FAMILY HISTORY

Not re-documented today; reviewed in prior consult notes.

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies. No prior reactions to contrast media.

MEDICATIONS

1. peme 500 mg/m2 IV day 1 q21 days (cycle 3 completed)
2. carbo AUC 5 IV day 1 q21 days (cycle 3 completed)
3. bev 15 mg/kg IV day 1 q21 days (cycle 3 completed)
4. Folic acid 400 mcg orally daily (Alimta supplementation)
5. Vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks (last administered prior to cycle 1)
6. Dexamethasone 4 mg IV pre-chemotherapy (day of infusion, per protocol)
7. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours PRN nausea (first 3 days post-infusion)
8. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally PRN breakthrough nausea
9. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily
10. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
11. Albuterol 90 mcg/actuation inhaler, 2 puffs PRN shortness of breath
12. Loratadine 10 mg daily PRN (allergic rhinitis)
13. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally PRN headache or pain

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, oriented, in no acute distress. Well-appearing woman who appears her stated age. More animated and interactive compared to the visit at time of progression. Daughter present at bedside.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, O2 saturation 96% on room air, temperature 36.8°C, weight [on file; stable from last visit].

ECOG Performance Status: 0-1 (patient reports feeling close to her baseline). Assessed as ECOG 1 given ongoing mild fatigue and the demands of her current treatment schedule.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Anicteric. Oropharynx clear without mucositis. Mild residual skin coarseness on the face from prior EGFR TKI-related rash, significantly improved from nadir. No significant paronychia involving fingernails; minimal residual bilateral first toe involvement.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No tracheal deviation.

Lungs: Mild expiratory wheeze at right upper zone, less prominent than at the progression visit. No egophony. Good air entry bilaterally. No dullness to percussion at the bases (the right-sided pleural effusion which was present at progression has resolved on the most recent CT, correlating with the improved exam findings).

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. No ascites. Normal bowel sounds.

Neurologic: Alert and fully oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Finger-nose testing normal, gait steady, no cerebellar signs. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Mini-cognitive screen not formally re-administered today; patient is engaging coherently in discussion without apparent difficulty.

Extremities: No significant clubbing. No lower extremity edema. Bilateral first toe paronychia minimal and improving.

Skin: Mild residual post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation on cheeks and forehead from prior acneiform rash; no active acneiform lesions. No petechiae or ecchymoses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC (obtained day of visit):
- WBC 5.2 x10^9/L (within normal limits)
- Absolute neutrophil count 3.1 x10^9/L (adequate)
- Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (mild anemia, improved from 10.4 g/dL at cycle 2 check; iron supplementation ongoing)
- Platelets 198 x10^9/L (adequate, no thrombocytopenia)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Creatinine 0.85 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2, adequate for Paraplatin and Avastin)
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
- ALT 28 U/L, AST 22 U/L (no hepatotoxicity)
- Albumin 3.8 g/dL
- Sodium, potassium, magnesium within normal limits

Urinalysis: Urine protein:creatinine ratio < 0.2 (acceptable for Avastin continuation -- no proteinuria)

HbA1c: 7.1% (diabetes remains adequately controlled, slight uptick likely related to intermittent dexamethasone steroid use on infusion days)

Coagulation: INR 1.0 (no coagulopathy)

Note: No CBC nadir obtained after cycle 3 was clinically concerning -- patient reported no febrile episodes, no bleeding, no significant fatigue at the expected nadir.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast (performed prior to this visit, reviewed today):
The right upper lobe primary mass has decreased from 3.2 cm (measured at progression on osi) to approximately 2.4 cm on the current scan. This is encouraging and confirms the chemotherapy regimen is having meaningful activity against the primary tumor. The right paratracheal lymph node has also gotten smaller, now measuring about 0.9 cm compared to 1.4 cm at the time of progression -- getting close to a normal size. The small right pleural effusion that we saw when she progressed is completely gone, which is another positive sign. Liver, adrenal glands, and bones look clean with no new concerning lesions.

MRI Brain with gadolinium contrast (performed concurrent with CT, reviewed today):
Both previously treated brain metastases are responding beautifully to the SRS. The right frontal lesion is down to about 0.6 cm from 1.1 cm, and the cerebellar lesion has nearly disappeared at 0.4 cm from 0.8 cm. There are no new brain lesions, which is very reassuring. The shrinkage could represent either true tumor response or radiation-induced changes -- at this point, either way, the trajectory is favorable and we do not need any additional intracranial interventions.

Overall impression on imaging: The chemotherapy is clearly doing something -- the primary tumor and nodes have shrunk and the pleural effusion has resolved. This is a partial response, and the trajectory looks stable to mildly improving. Brain is under very good control post-SRS.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IIIB EGFR L858R-MUTANT PULMONARY ADENOCARCINOMA, NOW WITH INTRACRANIAL METASTASES, STATUS POST SRS -- PARTIAL RESPONSE TO SECOND-LINE carbo/Alimta/Avastin

Ms. Yuen has done well with her second-line chemotherapy regimen and we are seeing a meaningful response in her chest and continued regression of her SRS-treated brain lesions. She has completed 3 of a planned 4-6 cycles of the induction chemotherapy phase. Given the response we are seeing and her overall good tolerance, the plan is to proceed with cycle 4 of carbo/Alimta/Avastin.

After completing induction, the current plan -- which is consistent with standard of care for non-squamous NSCLC with platinum-based chemotherapy induction -- is to transition to maintenance peme plus bev. We will continue this until disease progression or prohibitive toxicity. There are good data supporting this approach for non-squamous NSCLC including from the PARAMOUNT and ECOG 4599/PointBreak-type data that have informed this strategy. Her non-squamous (adenocarcinoma) histology and absence of bev-related hypertension or proteinuria make her an appropriate candidate.

We will schedule her next restaging imaging after cycle 4 is complete, or approximately 10-12 weeks from now total (end of the induction phase).

2. MANAGEMENT OF CHEMOTHERAPY-RELATED TOXICITIES

- Nausea/vomiting: Grade 1-2 pattern improving with current antiemetic regimen. Will continue ondansetron and dexamethasone on day 1 of each cycle. She may add prochlorperazine or lorazepam as breakthrough agents if needed days 2-3 post-infusion. Reinforce hydration.
- Fatigue: Grade 1-2, improving cycle-by-cycle. Encouraging moderate physical activity as tolerated, especially short walks. Social work will continue to provide support and connect her with community resources.
- Anemia: Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, improving with iron supplementation. Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally twice daily with vitamin C. Will re-check hemoglobin before cycle 4. If she drops below 8 g/dL or becomes symptomatic, will reassess need for transfusion or ESA (erythropoiesis-stimulating agent).
- bev monitoring: Blood pressure today 128/76, urine protein:creatinine ratio within acceptable range. Continue to monitor at each visit. Avastin cleared to continue.
- Alopecia: Grade 1, expected with carbo. No pharmacologic intervention; scalp cooling was discussed previously but patient declined. Reviewed resources for head coverings and wigs.
- peme supplementation: Folic acid daily and vitamin B12 IM every 9 weeks ongoing. Confirm next B12 injection timing with infusion center.

3. CNS DISEASE -- STATUS POST SRS, RESPONDING

The two treated brain metastases continue to respond. No new intracranial disease on today's MRI. Neurology and radiation oncology are following. Next dedicated brain MRI to be done with the cycle 4 restaging CT. No additional intracranial therapy planned at this time. She is off dexamethasone (no longer needed for intracranial edema). Will monitor for any new neurologic symptoms and act promptly if they develop.

4. DISCUSSION OF FUTURE THERAPY OPTIONS AND CLINICAL TRIAL OPPORTUNITIES

Given the nature of Ms. Yuen's disease -- EGFR L858R-mutant NSCLC with acquired resistance mechanisms including EGFR T790M and now confirmed MET amplification (on prior tissue NGS) -- she faces a challenging resistance landscape beyond the current chemotherapy regimen. We had an important conversation today with both Ms. Yuen and her daughter about what comes next.

I introduced the concept of clinical trial options that are becoming available for patients with exactly her molecular profile -- specifically, those who have progressed on an EGFR TKI and are now on or will have completed platinum-based chemotherapy. I discussed the rationale for dual EGFR/MET targeting given her MET amplification, which is a known bypass mechanism in EGFR-mutant NSCLC after Tagrisso. I also discussed the role of immunotherapy in this population, noting that her PD-L1 TPS was 28% on the most recent biopsy.

Our research coordinator was introduced to Ms. Yuen and her daughter today and will provide detailed information about a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody in combination with an anti-PD-L1 agent for patients with EGFR-mutant NSCLC after TKI and chemotherapy exposure. The patient and her daughter expressed genuine enthusiasm and asked several thoughtful questions about what participation would involve, including frequency of visits and potential side effects. Screening procedures were briefly outlined; formal eligibility screening will occur if and when she completes induction chemotherapy and moves toward trial enrollment. The research coordinator will call within the week to schedule a formal pre-screening visit.

I was transparent with Ms. Yuen that despite the good response she is having right now, there is a meaningful probability of eventual progression on the current regimen, and having a well-defined next step is important. She appreciated the forthright conversation.

5. DIABETES AND HYPERTENSION MANAGEMENT

HbA1c 7.1% -- slightly above her previous baseline of 6.9%, likely partially related to steroid use on infusion days. Will discuss with her primary care physician regarding monitoring. Metformin dose maintained. Blood pressure well-controlled today at 128/76 on lisinopril. No adjustments needed.

6. COPD MONITORING

No respiratory exacerbations during chemotherapy. O2 saturation 96% on room air, stable. Continued PRN albuterol. Advise flu and pneumococcal vaccines are up to date given immunosuppressive chemotherapy; will verify with nursing.

7. RETURN VISIT

Ms. Yuen will return in 3 weeks for cycle 4 of Paraplatin/pem/Avastin (day 1). Labs (CBC, CMP, urine protein:creatinine) to be drawn 24-48 hours prior to infusion per standard protocol. She understands to call the oncology triage line immediately for fever > 38.3°C, significant bleeding, severe shortness of breath, new neurologic symptoms, or other concerning changes.

She was seen today for approximately 40 minutes. All questions from the patient and her daughter were addressed. She expressed gratitude and left the appointment in good spirits.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Oncologist, MD]
Thoracic Medical Oncology Program
[Institution on file]
